document.title: Partial Risk Guarantee in Support of the Power Sector Privatisation — Project Appraisal Report

promises.note: Assumed from privatisation incentive — no binding targets set

prg_mechanism.instrument: Standby Letter of Credit backstopped by ADF PRG
prg_mechanism.coverage: 3 months of NBET payment obligations to each IPP
prg_mechanism.trigger_event: NBET non-payment to IPP under PPA

structural_gaps.detail: PRG protected IPP investors and commercial lenders. No equivalent mechanism protected household consumers. Document is structurally investor-first.
TCN failure to evacuate power was explicitly classified as a non-triggering risk (Table 4.1). If TCN failed, consumers received no benefit and the guarantee was irrelevant.

structural_gaps.gap: Gas supply excluded from PRG trigger
structural_gaps.detail: Pipeline vandalism and Niger Delta militancy also classified as non-triggering. The most probable cause of IPP output failure had no financial backstop.
Vulnerable household tariff mentioned in one sentence. No mechanism, amount, or implementation schedule provided.
Privatisation assumed to automatically incentivise billing and metering improvement. No binding targets, timelines, or sanctions specified.
NBET was 3 years old with no prior donor-funded project experience, yet was the linchpin of the entire payment architecture backing USD 180M in guarantees.
40,000 MW target by 2020 cited approvingly. System at 5,500 MW usable in 2013. Required 8× increase in 7 years. No credibility analysis in document.
Document explicitly states NERC's capacity to enforce tariff policy 'remains uncertain' — the consumer protection regulator had no guaranteed enforcement power.

policy_lineage.thread_consumer_cost: Consumers paid N60–N80/kWh generator costs from at least 2001 through 2013 while state built instruments to attract capital. Consumer bore the full cost of the institutional gap.

key_institutions.NBET: Nigeria Bulk Electricity Trading PLC — bulk purchaser and reseller of electricity from IPPs to DisCos
key_institutions.NERC: Nigeria Electricity Regulatory Commission — tariff enforcement, consumer protection
key_institutions.TCN: Transmission Company of Nigeria — single transmission entity, not privatised
key_institutions.PHCN: Power Holding Company of Nigeria — predecessor state monopoly, unbundled

tariff.description: 15-year tariff path designed to be cost-reflective and attract private investment

vitics_notes.document_character: Financial instrument, not policy. Operates entirely above the consumer layer. Benefits flow: IPP investors → national grid → consumers (contingent on 4 links all working).
vitics_notes.key_finding: The PRG reveals that 8 years after EPSRA 2005, the primary obstacle to reform delivery was government creditworthiness, not policy design. The consumer bore the full cost of that gap in generator fuel throughout.
vitics_notes.consumer_experience_gap: Document is silent on consumer experience between 2013 and promised 2016 delivery. No consumer redress mechanism if targets were missed.
vitics_notes.batch2_significance: First international financial backstop for Nigerian power reform. Sets the financial architecture within which all subsequent reform-era delivery must be evaluated.
